text: 6 Minute English From bbclearningenglish .com
zh: 6分钟英语，来自bbclearningenglish.com

text: Hello. This is 6 Minute English from BBC Learning English. I'm Pippa. And I'm Beth.
zh: 大家好，这里是BBC英语教学的《6分钟英语》节目。我是Pippa。我是Beth。

text: Foraging is the activity of walking in the countryside, looking for plants you can eat that grow in the wild.
zh: 觅食是指在乡间行走，寻找可以食用的野生植物的活动。

text: That's anything from blackberries and chestnuts to mushrooms and wild garlic.
zh: 这些植物种类繁多，从黑莓、栗子到蘑菇和野蒜都包括在内。

text: It's something humans have always done and recently it's become fashionable among groups of young people.
zh: 这是人类一直以来的行为，最近在年轻人群体中变得很流行。

text: Pippa, have you ever been foraging? Yes, we used to go and pick blackberries
zh: Pippa，你觅食过吗？是的，小时候我们经常去摘黑莓，

text: when I was younger but I haven't really done it as an adult
zh: 但长大后就没怎么做过了，

text: and I would be a bit nervous to look for mushrooms or things like that.
zh: 而且我会有点害怕去找蘑菇之类的东西。

text: What about you? you? I'm exactly the same. If I see a blackberry on a bush, I will eat it. But mushrooms, no, too scary.
zh: 你呢？我也是一样。如果我在灌木丛里看到黑莓，我会吃掉它。但蘑菇，不行，太吓人了。

text: So in this episode, we'll meet two young women from different countries who are passionate about foraging wild food.
zh: 所以在这一集里，我们将认识两位来自不同国家、对觅食野生食物充满热情的年轻女性。

text: They're both self -taught, having learned to forage by studying the natural world around them.
zh: 她们都是自学成才，通过研究周围的自然世界学会了觅食。

text: And it's important to say that they only eat what they can identify with 100 % certainty,
zh: 重要的是要说明，她们只吃自己能百分之百确定的食物，

text: sticking to the rule, if in doubt, leave it out.
zh: 坚持一个原则：如果有疑问，就不要吃。

text: We'll also be learning some useful new words and phrases, all of which you'll find on our website, bbclearningenglish .com.
zh: 我们还会学习一些有用的新单词和短语，你都可以在我们的网站bbclearningenglish.com上找到。

text: But before that, I have a question for you, Pippa.
zh: 但在那之前，我有一个问题要问你，Pippa。

text: As mentioned, when foraging, you must know for certain what is safe to eat.
zh: 正如刚才提到的，觅食时你必须确切知道什么是安全可食用的。

text: Something definitely not safe to eat is the mushroom death
zh: 有一种绝对不安全的蘑菇叫做死帽蕈。

text: cap. As the name suggests, it's one of the world's deadliest mushrooms, and it's common across the British Isles.
zh: 顾名思义，它是世界上最致命的蘑菇之一，在不列颠群岛很常见。

text: But what colour is it? Are death cap mushrooms A, brown, B, white, or C, red?
zh: 但它是什么颜色的呢？死帽蕈是A，棕色，B，白色，还是C，红色？

text: I'm going to say if it's dangerous, maybe it's red. Okay, well, we'll find out later in the program.
zh: 我猜如果它很危险，也许是红色。好吧，我们稍后在节目中揭晓答案。

text: First, let's meet Roshana Gray, a wild food forager living in Cape Point, South Africa.
zh: 首先，让我们认识一下Roshana Gray，她是一位居住在南非开普角的野生食物觅食者。

text: Roshana learned how to forage from her mother -in -law, as she explains here to BBC World Service Programme, The Conversation.
zh: Roshana是从她的婆婆那里学会觅食的，她在BBC世界服务节目《The Conversation》中这样解释道。

text: So she is a horticulturalist. She has a deep knowledge of plants.
zh: 她是一位园艺学家，对植物有着深厚的了解。

text: And so I would be kind of scanning the landscape and learning about these new plants or new to me.
zh: 所以我会环视整个地貌，去了解这些新的植物，或者说是对我来说是新的植物，

text: and wondering if you could eat them. Something that's incredibly important in foraging is to know 110 % if you can eat it.
zh: 并思考它们是否可以食用。在觅食中非常重要的一点是要百分之百确定你是否能吃它。

text: Positive identification of the plant. And so she was my plant person.
zh: 要对植物进行确切的鉴定。所以她就是我的植物老师。

text: Roshana's mother -in -law is a horticulturalist, a person who studies or grows plants such as flowers, fruits and vegetables.
zh: Roshana的婆婆是一位园艺学家，也就是研究或种植花卉、水果和蔬菜等植物的人。

text: She has a deep knowledge of the wild plants growing in nature.
zh: 她对自然中生长的野生植物有着深厚的了解。

text: A deep knowledge means a thorough understanding of a subject gained from experience and study.
zh: 深厚的了解意味着通过经验和学习获得的对某一主题的透彻理解，

text: not just knowing the basic facts. One skill foragers need is scanning the landscape.
zh: 而不仅仅是知道基本事实。觅食者需要具备的一项技能是环视地貌。

text: Scanning means searching a wide area with your eyes to find some particular thing. In this case, edible plants and herbs.
zh: 环视意味着用眼睛搜索一大片区域，以寻找某种特定的东西，在这里指的是可食用的植物和草本植物。

text: And of course, Roshana only eats plants that she can positively identify, plants that she knows 110 % are safe to eat.
zh: 当然，Roshana只吃她能确切辨认的植物，也就是她百分之百确定安全可食用的植物。

text: Here, the idiom 110 % means Roshana is completely sure, she has no doubt.
zh: 这里，习语“百分之百”的意思是Roshana完全确信，毫无疑问。

text: And that's important as the consequences of eating poisonous plants are serious,
zh: 这一点很重要，因为食用有毒植物的后果很严重，

text: sickness or even death. Our second female forager, Emily Smith,
zh: 可能是生病甚至死亡。我们的第二位女性觅食者Emily Smith，

text: moved to rural Japan to work on a project cataloguing and collecting wild mushrooms.
zh: 搬到了日本乡下，参与一个对野生蘑菇进行编目和采集的项目。

text: With around 5 ,000 varieties, 300
zh: 蘑菇大约有5000个品种，其中300种可以食用，

text: of which are edible, mushrooms are an important part of traditional Japanese cooking.
zh: 是日本传统烹饪中重要的组成部分。

text: But with names like death cap and a reputation for being poisonous,
zh: 但由于像死帽蕈这样的名字，以及有毒的名声，

text: mushrooms are what many wild foragers worry about the most.
zh: 蘑菇是许多野生觅食者最担心的东西。

text: Emily discussed these worries with BBC World Service programme The Conversation.
zh: Emily在BBC世界服务节目《The Conversation》中谈到了这些担忧。

text: That stigma is a bit misplaced, I think, because plants can be just as dangerous, if not more dangerous than mushrooms.
zh: 我认为这种污名有点不公平，因为有些植物可能和蘑菇一样危险，甚至更危险。

text: And I think that mushrooms have been much maligned.
zh: 而且我觉得蘑菇一直被过分诋毁了。

text: I mean, you've got to exercise caution, as Roshana said, in anything that you pick.
zh: 我是说，正如Roshana所说，对任何你采摘的东西都要保持谨慎。

text: You need to know what it is before you consume it. If in doubt, throw it out is a very good phrase to say.
zh: 在食用之前，你需要知道它到底是什么。‘如果有疑问，就丢掉’是一句很好的话。

text: Emily thinks there's a stigma around mushrooms.
zh: Emily认为人们对蘑菇存在一种污名化的看法。

text: A stigma is a negative belief about something which is not necessarily true or fair.
zh: 污名是一种关于某事物的负面看法，但这种看法不一定真实或公正。

text: In fact, many plants are just as poisonous as mushrooms.
zh: 事实上，许多植物和蘑菇一样有毒。

text: The main thing is to exercise caution, to act with care and attention so as to avoid possible dangers.
zh: 最重要的是要保持谨慎，也就是小心行事、多加留意，以避免可能的危险。

text: In other words, to be careful. And the best way of doing that is learning the difference between what's safe to eat and what's not.
zh: 换句话说，就是要小心。而做到这一点的最好方法就是学会分辨什么安全可食用，什么不可以。

text: Speaking of which, what was the answer to your question, Beth? Ah, I asked you what colour are death cap mushrooms?
zh: 说到这个，Beth，你那个问题的答案是什么？啊，我问你死帽蕈是什么颜色的。

text: You said red, which I also thought was the answer, but actually they are white.
zh: 你说是红色，我原本也以为是这个答案，但实际上它们是白色的。

text: Right, it's time to recap the vocabulary we've learnt about foraging,
zh: 好了，是时候来回顾一下我们学到的关于觅食的词汇了，

text: starting with horticulturalist, a person who studies or grows plants.
zh: 首先是园艺学家，指研究或种植植物的人。

text: If you have a deep knowledge of a subject, you have a thorough understanding of it gained from experience and study.
zh: 如果你对某个主题有深厚的了解，就意味着你通过经验和学习获得了透彻的理解。

text: Scanning involves searching a wide area with your eyes to find some particular thing.
zh: 环视是指用眼睛搜索一大片区域，以寻找某个特定的东西。

text: If you're 110 % sure about something, you're absolutely certain, you have no doubt.
zh: 如果你对某件事百分之百确定，那就意味着你完全确信，毫无疑问。

text: out. A stigma is an unfair negative belief about something.
zh: 污名是指对某事物一种不公正的负面看法。

text: And finally, to exercise caution is a more formal way of saying to be careful
zh: 最后，“exercise caution”是“小心”的一种更正式的说法，

text: – something you should definitely remember if you go wild foraging
zh: 如果你打算自己去野外觅食，这一点你一定要记住。

text: yourself. Once again, our six minutes are up.
zh: 我们的六分钟又到时间了。

text: If you enjoyed this episode, you'll find a quiz and worksheet to practice the vocabulary on our website bbclearningenglish
zh: 如果你喜欢这一集，可以在我们的网站bbclearningenglish.com上找到练习词汇的测验和练习题。

text: .com. See you again soon. Bye for now. Goodbye. Bye!
zh: 我们下次再见。再见啦。拜拜！
